risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On completion of the baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe how the random sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks, coin toss). Under Cochrane guidance, 'randomly assigned' alone without further method details is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, and there is no evidence of a non-random method.

Quote: "On completion of the baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)." Comment: The article states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central randomization, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or equivalent). Therefore, whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the next assignment is not reported.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On completion of the baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)." and "A Powerpoint presentation was used to present definitions..." / "The facilitator presented information from published literature about the importance of considering ethnic cultural sensitivity..." Comment: Participants and workshop facilitators necessarily knew which workshop was being delivered/received because the two interventions were overtly different training sessions. The main outcomes were knowledge, recognition/intent to report, and clinical expertise measured immediately post-workshop, including self-reported likelihood of reporting in vignettes; these outcomes are plausibly influenced by awareness of allocation and expectations about the training received. Therefore lack of blinding could affect outcomes.

Quote: "On completion of the baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity). ... After completion of the workshops, participants were readministered the outcome measures in random order." Quote: "Respondents indicated their likelihood of reporting suspected child maltreatment from highly unlikely to highly likely on a 7-point Likerttype scale..." Comment: The outcome assessor for the main outcomes was the participant (self-report/self-completed questionnaires). Participants necessarily knew which workshop they received when completing posttest measures, so outcome assessment was not blinded. At least one outcome was subjective/judgement-dependent—the vignette-based recognition/intent-to-report measure using a 7-point likelihood rating—so knowledge of allocation could influence measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, this makes detection bias high risk overall, even though some knowledge-test items were more objective.

Quote: "The participants were 55 mental health professionals... One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment." and Table 2: "Pre and Posttest Scores... (N = 54)" Comment: Outcome data were missing for 1 of 55 randomized participants at the immediate postworkshop assessment (~1.8% attrition overall). This is essentially complete follow-up, and the analyzed sample was balanced across groups (Table 1 indicates 27 participants per arm in the analyzed sample). The reported reason was inability to complete the postworkshop assessment, with no suggestion that missingness was related to outcome or differed importantly by arm.
